summary.title: Zero-denominator guard is well-intentioned but produces a silently incorrect result for non-zero numerators
summary.body: The change adds a guard that returns 0.0 when denominator == 0.0. This prevents division-by-zero (which in IEEE 754 produces Inf or -Inf for non-zero numerators, and NaN for 0/0), but does so by unconditionally returning 0.0 regardless of the numerator. This masks the error condition and can produce misleading results downstream.
summary.analysis: The diff is a single-file, 3-line addition. The floating-point check `denominator == 0.0` is technically correct for exact zero (including -0.0) but does not guard against underflow-to-zero or extremely small denominators that could still overflow to infinity. The unconditional return of 0.0 is the primary concern: a caller expecting Inf for a non-zero numerator will silently receive 0.0, which can propagate incorrect calculations. In domain contexts where 'ratio' means e.g. a percentage with an empty baseline, returning 0.0 may be acceptable — but this cannot be verified from the diff alone.
residual_risk: Callers of ratio() may have relied on Inf/NaN propagation for error detection; the silent 0.0 return breaks that contract.
Near-zero but non-zero denominators (e.g., f64::MIN_POSITIVE) are not guarded and could still overflow.
Without access to the caller sites or test suite, the actual runtime impact is unknown.

findings.title: Unconditional zero return silences division-by-zero for non-zero numerators
findings.description: When denominator is zero and numerator is non-zero, the function returns 0.0 instead of the IEEE 754 result (Inf or -Inf). For numerator 0.0 / 0.0, it returns 0.0 instead of NaN. Both behaviors replace a well-defined floating-point error signal with an arbitrary sentinel value that downstream code may misinterpret.
findings.evidence: Diff line: `if denominator == 0.0 { return 0.0; }` — the guard does not distinguish numerator cases.

findings.title: Exact zero check does not protect against underflow or near-zero overflow
findings.description: The comparison `denominator == 0.0` catches exact zero (and -0.0) but will not catch extremely small non-zero values such as f64::MIN_POSITIVE, which can still produce Inf when used as a divisor with sufficiently large numerators.
findings.evidence: Diff line: `if denominator == 0.0` — equality on f64 misses values that are epsilon-close to zero.

comments.body: If the domain semantics of `ratio` intentionally treat an empty/zero baseline as producing zero, consider documenting this contract and adding a test to make it explicit. Alternatively, consider returning NaN for 0/0 and using `is_finite()` checks at call sites to handle the error case explicitly.

receipts.summary: Inspected the unified diff for src/ratio.rs; no additional repo files, tests, or runtime evidence available. Two findings raised: one major (silent zero return masks non-zero-numerator division-by-zero) and one minor (exact-zero check misses near-zero overflow).
